findings.title: createUser returns Promise<any> despite a User interface being defined in the same file

evidence.summary: Function signature says Promise<any> but the file defines a User interface and the implementation casts to User
diff_refs: +export async function createUser(request: APIRequestContext, email: string): Promise<any> {
+  return response.json() as unknown as User;

findings.why_it_matters: The return type Promise<any> defeats TypeScript's type checking for all callers. The User interface is defined right above this function — using Promise<User> as the return type is a one-character change that gives callers type safety.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the return type to `Promise<User>` and remove the `as unknown as User` cast: `const data: User = await response.json(); return data;`

findings.title: getUsers returns Promise<any> with no type annotation for the response
findings.why_it_matters: Returns an untyped array. Since a User interface exists in the file, the return type should be Promise<User[]>.
findings.suggested_fix: Change return type to `Promise<User[]>` and add type annotation: `const data: User[] = await response.json(); return data;`

findings.title: deleteUser parameter id typed as any instead of number

evidence.summary: deleteUser(request, id: any) — id should be number to match User.id
diff_refs: +export async function deleteUser(request: APIRequestContext, id: any): Promise<any> {

findings.why_it_matters: Typing id as any means TypeScript cannot catch passing a string or object as the user ID. User.id is typed as number — deleteUser should accept number.
findings.suggested_fix: Change signature to `deleteUser(request: APIRequestContext, id: number): Promise<boolean>` and return `response.ok()` typed as boolean.
